coredata.dc:title: Origin of sonocatalytic activity of fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Weijie
Wang, Huiqi
Chang, Qing
Li, Ning
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Although ultrasound-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) have believed to be primary intermediates for the realization of sonodynamic therapy and sonocatalytic reactions, the mechanism of ROS production under ultrasound has not been well clarified till now. Here we discovered that ultrasound cavitation can break up oxygen-containing groups on the surface of carbon dots (CDs) to generate ROS, giving rise to a novel mechanism different from as-reported those. The bond cleavage of oxygen-containing groups contribute transient oxygen free radicals (•O−) that involve in the subsequent formation of ROS by quickly reacting with hydrogen ions and water, thereby leading to a twice higher sonocatalytic activity of CDs than TiO2. Moreover, the sonosensitizer function of CDs can be tuned by pH values of the surrounding environment that determine the generation and evolution reaction of oxygen free radicals under ultrasound irradiation. This finding paves a new way to design multifunctional sonosensitizers and utilize them in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Reactive oxygen species
Sonocatalytic mechanism
Oxygen free radicals

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province , Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082), Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP) . We also thank our collaborator Dr. Adrian Trinchi of the CSIRO Australia for the discussion on this work.

This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082), Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP). We also thank our collaborator Dr. Adrian Trinchi of the CSIRO Australia for the discussion on this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sonocatalytic activities of TiO2, CDs and no sonosensitizer (a) and the effects on sonocatalytic activities of CDs (b–d): temperature (b), US power (c) and CDs' amount (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectra of CDs suspension and TA solution with and without CDs after US irradiation for 10 min; (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of NBT solution over CDs after US irradiation for 10 min; (c) Degradation efficiency of RhB over CDs before and after adding •OH, •O2 − and h+ radical scavengers, respectively; (d) Schematic illustration of the band structures of CDs at different pH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) FTIR spectra of CDs with and without US irradiation for 30 min; (b) The pH variation of CDs solution with US irradiation time; (c) PL and PLE spectra of CDs solution after different irradiation time; (d) The recyclability of CDs for RhB degradation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic of selective deactivation of hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups on the surface of CDs; (b) Comparisons of sonocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB using CDs, BA@CDs and BrPE@CDs as sonosensitizer, respectively. (c) Illustration of the sonocatalytic mechanism of CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The degradation efficiency of RhB over CDs in different pH conditions; (b) The degradation efficiency of RhB over CDs in the presence of •OH and •O2 − radical scavengers; (c) The pH increments of CDs solution in alkaline conditions with US irradiation time; (d) FTIR spectra of US-treated CDs at three pH conditions; (e and f) XPS C1s and O1s spectra of US-treated CDs at pH = 3 and pH = 11 conditions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The reported representative mechanisms of ROS production induced by US.

head.ce:title: Origin of sonocatalytic activity of fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, P. R. China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although ultrasound-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) have believed to be primary intermediates for the realization of sonodynamic therapy and sonocatalytic reactions, the mechanism of ROS production under ultrasound has not been well clarified till now. Here we discovered that ultrasound cavitation can break up oxygen-containing groups on the surface of carbon dots (CDs) to generate ROS, giving rise to a novel mechanism different from as-reported those. The bond cleavage of oxygen-containing groups contribute transient oxygen free radicals (•O−) that involve in the subsequent formation of ROS by quickly reacting with hydrogen ions and water, thereby leading to a twice higher sonocatalytic activity of CDs than TiO2. Moreover, the sonosensitizer function of CDs can be tuned by pH values of the surrounding environment that determine the generation and evolution reaction of oxygen free radicals under ultrasound irradiation. This finding paves a new way to design multifunctional sonosensitizers and utilize them in various fields.

The oxygen-containing groups upon carbon dots can be transformed into reactive oxygen species (ROS) under ultrasound irradiation.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasound (US) is a kind of periodic mechanical sound wave with a frequency beyond the limit of human hearing (>20 kHz) [1]. Due to its easy operation, deep penetration depth and high security, US has been widely utilized to sonodynamic therapy (SDT) and the removal of industrial organic pollutants by activating sonosensitizers to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) [2,3]. At present, two representative and plausible mechanisms i.e. sonoluminescence and pyrolysis, are always used to describe the ROS generation under US (Scheme 1 ) [4–6]. Specifically, the gas bubbles induced cavitation effect around the surface of sonosensitizers can break up to generate sonoluminescence under US irradiation. By its excitation, the sonosensitizer could induce electron–hole pairs with subsequent generation of ROS in the solution like photocatalysis [4,7–9]. During the collapse of gas bubbles induced by cavitation effect, the resulted high local temperature could cause water pyrolysis to generate •OH radicals serving as the second mechanism [10,11]. However, the above two mechanisms remain elusive for understanding the formation processes of radical species.

Under US irradiation, the nanoparticles e.g. TiO2, ZnO as sonosensitizers offer more nucleation sites for the formation of cavitation bubbles through reducing the cavitation threshold. After undergoing expansion, compression and impulsive collapse, the resultant cavitation bubbles by nanoparticles convert into the energy finally and then transfer heat (up to 10 000 K) and pressure (81 MPa) to the surrounding microenvironment [2]. Since the nanoparticles lie in the center of cavitation bubbles, their surface as well as the surface adsorbate will be subjected to the impact of extreme heat and pressure induced by cavitation effect. This phenomenon may result in breaking and making chemical bonds on the surface of nanoparticles, triggering the formation of radical species upon bubble collapse. Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical fluorescent nanoparticles with dimensions smaller than 10 nm and consist of graphitized sp2 carbon cores that are surrounded by the outer shells containing rich groups such as –COOH, –OH, C–O–C and –NH2 [12,13]. If the CDs serve as sonosensitizers, the outer shell of them is first confronted with the shock of cavitation bubbles under US irradiation. Thus US-generated extreme conditions could break apart the groups on the surface of CDs, giving rise to transient active species capable of ROS production by quickly reacting with surrounding environment. CDs have found numerous applications in photocatalysis, sensors, drug delivery, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices and nanocomposites due to their unique properties and excellent biocompatibility [14–17]. Particularly, the CDs including of graphene quantum dots (GQD) has been demonstrated to be a photosensitizer that can convert the light energy to generate ROS, e.g. singlet oxygen (1O2), for photodynamic therapy and antibacterial [18,19]. But as far as we're concerned no one reports their properties of the conversion of US energy into the production of ROS. Since US and light possess different behaviors in material excitation, the mechanism of ROS formation could be different as well.

In this contribution, we first discovered that the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs can be transformed into ROS under US irradiation. A kind of transient active species named as oxygen free radicals (•O−) could be generated during the collapse of cavitation bubbles and subsequently induces rapid transformation reactions from •O− to •OH and •O2 − with H+ and H2O, promoting ROS production. More importantly, these transformation reactions are reversible and can be tuned by the pH of aqueous solution, thereby hopeful to realize the control of ROS yield in the microenvironment. Comparing with the typical TiO2 sonosensitizer, the CDs exhibited nearly twice the sonocatalytic activity of it under the same conditions due to the contribution of surface groups.

ROS as reaction species not only cause cytotoxicity and genotoxicity in biology [11], but are also important for the redox reactions in catalysis, for instance, advanced oxidation processes in which organic pollutants in the wastewater can be removed [20]. Rhodamine-B (RhB), a representative organic dye pollutant, was chosen here as the reaction substrate to explore the sonocatalytic activity of CDs. Fig. 1 a shows RhB degradation with US irradiation time in the presence of commercial P25 TiO2 and CDs as well as no any sonosensitizer. The CDs exhibit good sonocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB under US: a 55% degradation efficiency is reached after 30 min whereas with TiO2 only 35% degradation is achieved. The sonocatalytic degradation efficiency of CDs for RhB is nearly 200% times higher than that of TiO2 (Fig. S1). Most interestingly, increasing the incipient temperature of reaction solution can boost sonocatalytic activity of CDs (Fig. 1b), whereas enhancing US power shows little effect on their activity (Fig. 1c). It should be mentioned that water circulating cooling system was employed in reaction process under US irradiation. Under this case, a very little change of reaction solution temperature was observed (Fig. S2a). If the circulating cooling system was not used in the experiment, however, the temperature of reaction solution rose much higher similar with the results reported in literature (Fig. S2b) [21]. Fig. 1d provides the influence of the added amount of CDs on RhB degradation under US irradiation. It can be found that the increase of CDs amount can effectively enhance the degradation - efficiency of RhB since more cavitation bubbles could be produced under US. Besides, we explored the sonocatalytic oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in the presence of CDs as well. From Fig. S3, UV–Vis absorption spectra clearly display the characteristic absorption peaks of the oxidation productions of TMB [22] and OPD [23], indicating that the CDs can also promote the oxidation of TMB and OPD under US irradiation.

According to the reaction of •OH radicals with terephthalic acid (TA) to generate 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH) with fluorescence emission at around 426 nm, we detected whether •OH radicals produced or not in the presence of CDs under US [23,24]. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, obvious fluorescent signals associated with TAOH are generated upon US irradiation of CDs suspended in 3 mM TA solution containing 10 mM NaOH. This verifies the production of •OH radicals when US irradiates the suspension of CDs. As a result of the reduction by •O2 − radicals the nitro blue tetrazorium (NBT) can be converted into blue formazan [25]. Based on this, NBT can be employed to detect •O2 −radicals produced by US irradiation via UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometer. From Fig. 2b, the NBT characteristic absorption peak at 250 nm significantly decreases after 10 min US irradiation, suggesting the formation of abundant •O2 − radicals using CDs as sonosensitizers. Under no US irradiation, however, CDs are not able to degrade RhB dyes as well as to produce any ROS (Fig. S4). As such, it can be safely deduced that the ROS formation is attributed to US treatment for CDs.

To confirm the role of these radicals in the sonocatalytic process, isopropanol (IPA), ascorbic acid (VC) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) were used as scavengers to interact with •OH, •O2 − and holes (h+), respectively [25–27]. In the presence of IPA and VC, the sonocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB for CDs reduces remarkably, while the addition of EDTA-2Na can't effectively inhibit the degradation of RhB (Fig. 2c). To determine whether 1O2 species could contribute to the degradation of RhB or not, l-histidine (L-his) as a 1O2 scavenger was added the reaction system without changing other conditions [28]. As shown in Fig. S5, the RhB degradation rate is almost unchanged, implying that 1O2 species are not the main oxidative specie and/or are inexistent in our presented reaction system. Unlike with functionalized fullerenes reported previously [29,30], consequently the sonocatalytic activity of CDs is mainly attributed to both •OH and •O2 − radicals.

One of reasons that EDTA-2Na as h+ scavenger has little effect on RhB degradation may be relatively small output of h+ resulted from sonoluminescence. To confirm it, the energy band structure diagrams of CDs in different pH conditions were determined by valence-band spectra (Fig. S6) of x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and the band gaps estimated by Tauc plot [31] (Fig. S7). As illustrated in Fig. 2d, the conduction-band minimum (CBM) of CDs lies the underneath of redox potential for O2/•O2 − (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) [32] and decreases with pH increase. Therefore, •O2 − radicals are difficult to be produced via CDs by means of sonoluminescence induced by US. From Fig. 2d, the redox potential for •OH/OH− is above the valence-band maximum (VBM). This means that the sonoluminescence mechanism may contribute •OH production. Unexpectedly, the CDs exhibit lower sonocatalytic activity under the alkaline condition (Fig. S8). By characterization for photoluminescence (PL) behaviors of CDs in different pH conditions, one can observe that PL intensities of CDs’ solution gradually enhance with the rise of pH (Fig. S9). In the meantime, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs clearly shorten as pH increases (Fig. S10 and Table S1). Consequently, CDs tend to incur radiative recombination rather than charge separation and transfer in the alkaline condition [26,33,34]. Many efforts have proven that PL centers of CDs originate from energy traps induced surface groups, thus the reaction of surface groups with H+ and OH− could cause the variation of their electronic structures, endowing CDs with different photogenerated charge behaviors [35–38].

By the selective reactions of –OH and –COOH groups with benzoic anhydride (BA) and 2-bromo-1-phenylethanone (BrPE) illustrated in Fig. 4 a, respectively, BA- and BrPE-modified CDs (namely CDs@BA and CDs@BrPE) were achieved [40–42]and further used as sonosensitizers to conduct sonocatalytic degradation of RhB. Fig. 4b gives the comparisons of the sonocatalytic activities of CDs, CDs@BA and CDs@BrPE samples under the same conditions. The activity of CDs@BA or CDs@BrPE is much lower than that of CDs, revealing the –OH and –COOH groups on the surface of CDs are important for the sonocatalytic activity. Since the sonocatalytic activity of CDs is associated with the breakage of surface oxygen-containing groups, we propose the following mechanism of ROS formation from CDs under US. When appropriate US irradiates CDs in reaction solution, it causes cavitation in which involves the nucleation, growth, and impulsive collapse of gas bubbles [2,8]. The extreme heat and pressure induced by cavitation effect give rise to a strong shattering effect [2], which makes the surface of CDs suffer from an explosive shock. The oxygen-containing groups together with their hydrogen-bonded water could be split up into intermediate species, such as •O-, •OH, H+ and others (Fig. 4c). Subsequently, the fast reactions between the active species (e.g. •O-, •OH) and the ambient liquid take place as follows:
(1)
･
O −
+
H +
→ ･ O H
(2)
3 ･
O −
+
H 2
O → 2 O
H −
+ ･
O 2 −
(3)
･ O H + O
H −
⇆ ･
O −
+
H 2
O
(4)
2 ･
O −
+ ･ O H → O
H −
+ ･
O 2 −

Even if C–C/CC bonds on the carbon skeleton can be broken under US-induced explosive shock, the formed intermediate species are also difficult to be evolved into ROS like the above reaction processes. As thus, only the bond cleavage of oxygen-containing groups can result in the production of ROS through sonication.

Oxygen free radical •O− as a transient species is so unstable that it is hard to be trapped by the general detection methods towards ROS [43]. Nevertheless, it can react with H+ and/or H2O to generate •OH and •O2 − on the basis of reactions (1) and (2), respectively, thus enhancing catalytic activity under US. The reactions (1) and (2) are seen to be decided by the concentration of H+ ions. In other words, both reactions easily proceed in acidic conditions while are inhibited in alkaline conditions since the H+ and OH− are the reactant in the reaction (1) and the product in the reaction (2), respectively. Fig. 5 a presents the sonocatalytic activities of CDs in the solution with different pH values. The degradation efficiency of RhB is extremely higher in acidic conditions compared to those in alkaline conditions. Similarly, the acidic conditions are able to facilitate the interaction of both •O− and •OH to produce •O2 − based on the reaction (4), thereby the effects of •O2 − on sonocatalytic activities of CDs may depend on pH. To confirm it, we employed scavengers to interact with •OH and •O2 − generated in different pH solution during RhB degradation process under US. From Fig. 5b, the degradation efficiency of RhB in the presence of VC decreases markedly with pH decline, whereas the addition of IPA does not show a similar trend with the effect of VC. Apart from by reaction (1), the production of •OH can derive from sonoluminescence and water pyrolysis reported previously [8,44]. As a result, the effects of •OH on sonocatalytic activities of CDs are independent on pH values. For reaction (3), it only takes place in alkaline conditions and is reversible. When its reverse reaction occurs during US process, it may be observed a signal that the pH of the reaction solution containing CDs increases. Fig. 5c plots the relationship between the pH increment of CDs’ solution and US irradiation time in alkaline conditions, indicating that the reaction between •O− and H2O exists in US process.

To further uncover how the evolution of ROS is intervened by pH under US, FTIR and XPS were used to quantitate the chemical structure changes on the surface of CDs after US irradiation in different pH conditions. The FTIR spectra of the CDs from pH ≤ 7 conditions show that the stretching vibration peak at 1714 cm−1 decreases while the accompanying peak at 1620 cm−1 increases (Fig. 5d). Meanwhile, the peak assigning with C–O groups disappears. By contrast, only one strong peak at 1620 cm−1 is present in FTIR spectra of the CDs from the condition of pH = 11 due to the reaction of the –COOH with NaOH. Particularly, the vibration peak of C–O groups is still observed obviously. Fig. 5e shows XPS C1s spectra of CDs at pH = 3 and pH = 11. The peak at around 289 eV for the CDs from pH = 3 condition is lower than that of the CDs from pH = 11 condition. From XPS O1s spectra shown in Fig. 5f, the peak associating with C–O groups of the CDs from pH = 3 condition reduces significantly, whereas the peak of CO groups enhances comparing with that of the CDs from pH = 11 condition. The above results reveal that the loss of surface groups of CDs resulted from US cavitation relies on the pH of reaction solution. The changes of pH may incur two consequences during US process: 1) The electric double layer at the solid-liquid interface induced by the interaction of a great number of cations (e.g. Na+) with CDs influences the cleavage of surface groups [24]; 2) The stability of •O− radicals gets more worse without protonation protection, inhibiting their formation.

In conclusion, we have examined the sonocatalytic process of CDs as sonosensitizers. Unlike with the reported mechanisms of sonoluminescence and pyrolysis, the formation of ROS mainly derives from the cleavage of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs caused by US cavitation effect. The •O− radicals have been demonstrated to be produced during US process and can react with H+ and/or H2O to generate •OH and •O2 −, lead to high yield of ROS. Comparing with the typical TiO2 sonosensitizer, the CDs exhibit nearly twice the sonocatalytic activity of it under the same conditions due to the contribution of surface groups. Moreover, the special reaction between the •O− radicals and the ambient liquid highly relies on pH values. Therefore, our findings may pave a new way to design multifunctional sonosensitizers and utilize them in various fields.

Weijie Ren: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Huiqi Wang: Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Qing Chang: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Ning Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Jinlong Yang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Shengliang Hu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082), Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP). We also thank our collaborator Dr. Adrian Trinchi of the CSIRO Australia for the discussion on this work.
